Yes, that would be possible, but sometimes not easy: for example, a Arc::drop ends up into a real drop of the inner object. If you put every Arc::drop into workqueue, then it's somewhat overkilling.

What I did was

  • making FnTimer the owner of the timeout closure,
  • and the timeout closure is a FnMut instead of FnOnce,
  • and it won't drop after executed in timer callbacks
  • instead it will drop when FnTimer is drop.

Being a FnMut also makes it work for repeatable timers (see the struct Next) and example

You can use this patch of mine, which will be part of the binder dependencies:

commit 4788570387b298917db8c84bcfe86b6e48b471a0
Author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>
Date:   Sat Oct 28 18:45:08 2023 +0200

    sync: add `Arc::into_unique_or_drop`

    Sometimes you might want to destroy a reference to a refcounted object
    without running the risk of running the destructor (e.g., maybe you are
    in an atomic context, but the destructor locks a mutex). This method
    allows you to do that, while handling the case where the refcount drops
    to zero separately.

    This can be used to implement methods similar to `mmput_async` that will
    run the destructor on a workqueue instead of running it immediately.

    Signed-off-by: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs b/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs
index 77cdbcf7bd2e..92b124d9b7cc 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs
@@ -273,6 +273,35 @@ pub fn as_arc_borrow(&self) -> ArcBorrow<'_, T> {
     pub fn ptr_eq(this: &Self, other: &Self) -> bool {
         core::ptr::eq(this.ptr.as_ptr(), other.ptr.as_ptr())
     }
+
+    /// Converts this [`Arc`] into a [`UniqueArc`], or destroys it if it is not unique.
+    ///
+    /// This method properly avoids races, so when this destroys the `Arc`, it never runs the
+    /// destructor of the value.
+    pub fn into_unique_or_drop(self) -> Option<Pin<UniqueArc<T>>> {
+        // We will manually manage the refcount in this method, so we disable the destructor.
+        let me = ManuallyDrop::new(self);
+        // SAFETY: The `Arc` cannot be destroyed during this method, so this is safe.
+        let refcount = unsafe { me.ptr.as_ref() }.refcount.get();
+
+        // SAFETY: If the refcount reaches a non-zero value, then we have destroyed this `Arc` and
+        // will return without running its destructor. If the refcount reaches zero, then there are
+        // no other arcs, and we can create a `UniqueArc`.
+        let is_zero = unsafe { bindings::refcount_dec_and_test(refcount) };
+        if is_zero {
+            // SAFETY: We have exclusive access to the arc, so we can perform unsynchronized
+            // accesses to the refcount.
+            unsafe { core::ptr::write(refcount, bindings::REFCOUNT_INIT(1)) };
+
+            // SAFETY: We own one refcount, so we can create a `UniqueArc`. It needs to be pinned,
+            // since an `Arc` is pinned.
+            unsafe {
+                Some(Pin::new_unchecked(UniqueArc { inner: Arc::from_inner(me.ptr) }))
+            }
+        } else {
+            None
+        }
+    }
 }

 impl<T: 'static> ForeignOwnable for Arc<T> {

So I just (ab)use that fact and make closures don't get dropped in callbacks. Even with this, users can do more fine grain control with the Arc::into_uniqure_or_drop.

Hm, are you talking about something like this?

     /// Acquire a [`TimerHandle`] for this timer
    pub fn get_handle(&self, receiver: T::Receiver) -> TimerHandle<T>
    where
        T: HasTimer<T>
    {
        if self.handle_count.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed) == 0 {
            // Since we cancel the timer upon dropping the last TimerHandle, but only do so after
            // decrementing `handle_count`: the destruction isn't atomic and thus it is entirely
            // possible that the thread which destroyed the last TimerHandle may not have completed
            // yet. In this case, it would be possible for the following sequence of events to
            // occur:
            //
            // Thread 1:                                  Thread 2:
            // * TimerHandle::drop() -> .handle_count = 0
            // *           --- PRE-EMPTED ---             * Timer::get_handle() -> .handle_count = 1
            //                                            * TimerHandle::schedule(...)
            // * TimerHandle::cancel(...)                 *           --- PRE-EMPTED ---
            //
            // Which would lead to schedule() becoming an unexpected no-op. Thus - if we are the
            // first handle to be taken out, synchronize by calling `hrtimer_cancel` to wait for any
            // ongoing handle cleanups to finish.
            //
            // SAFETY: Since `self.timer` was initialized by `hrtimer_init()`, it is valid via the C
            // API contract to call `hrtimer_cancel` here
            unsafe { bindings::hrtimer_cancel(self.timer.get()) };
        }

        TimerHandle(receiver)
    }

impl<T> Drop for TimerHandle<T>
where
    T: HasTimer<T> + TimerCallback
{
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        // This is the only existing handle for the timer, so cancel the timer now that we're
        // dropping it since we cannot guarantee its memory won't be freed after our contents are
        // dropped
        if self.get_timer().handle_count.fetch_sub(1, Ordering::Relaxed) == 1 {
            self.cancel();
        }
    }
}

  • We need to make sure the timer callback doesn't access the object when the object gets dropped. (this is due to the first one).

I'm looking into this now. Here is a recap of the thread:

Key points

  • If we explicitly drop the target in the timer callback, we have a dangling reference

  • If the callback owns the last reference to the target, target will be dropped
    in non-sleepable context when the callback is finished.

  • When dropping an object that is the target of an armed timer, we may drop
    fields accessed by the timer callback before we cancel the timer (drop order).

  • Handle approach - handle owns object rather than callback

    • Knowing if we should stop the timer when we drop the last handle. There can be
      multiple handles to the same timer.

    • If we make schedule fallible, we can assert a single handle

    • What happens if we start the timer multiple times?
    • Cancel before starting?

Option 1

Make RawTimer the owner of the target and RawTimer::drop means 1) cancel the
timer and 2) drop the ownership of the target object.

Option 2

Make RawTimer::schedule return a handle type and the drop of that handle
type cancels the timer and release ownership of the target object.

Option 3

If drop of target happens after timer callback, schedule drop on workqueue.

#[pin_data]
pub struct ClosureTimer<T> {
    #[pin]
    timer: Timer<ClosureTimer<T>>,
    #[pin]
    callback: Mutex<Option<T>>,
}

impl_has_timer! {
    impl{T} HasTimer<Self> for ClosureTimer<T> { self.timer }
}

impl<T> TimerCallback for ClosureTimer<T>
where
    T: FnOnce() + 'static,
{
    type CallbackTarget<'a> = Pin<Box<ClosureTimer<T>>>;
    type CallbackTargetParameter<'a> = &'a ClosureTimer<T>;

    fn run(this: Self::CallbackTargetParameter<'_>) -> TimerRestart
    where
        Self: Sized,
    {
        if let Some(callback) = this.callback.lock().take() {
            callback();
        }
        TimerRestart::NoRestart
    }
}

impl<T> ClosureTimer<T>
where
    T: FnOnce() + 'static,
    T: Send,
    T: Sync,
{
    fn new(f: T, flags: Flags) -> Result<Pin<Box<Self>>> {
        Box::pin_init(
            pin_init!(
                Self {
                    timer <- Timer::new(),
                    callback <- new_mutex!(Some(f)),
                }
            ),
            flags,
        )
    }
}

/// Start a timer that executes `f` after `expires` time.
pub fn start_function<T>(expires: Ktime, f: T) -> Result<BoxTimerHandle<ClosureTimer<T>>>
where
    T: FnOnce() + 'static,
    T: Send,
    T: Sync,
{
    let timer = ClosureTimer::<T>::new(f, flags::GFP_KERNEL)?;
    let handle = <Pin<Box<ClosureTimer<T>>> as TimerPointer>::start(timer, expires);
    Ok(handle)
}

JFYI: this was where I originally got this from:

static bool vkms_get_vblank_timestamp(struct drm_crtc *crtc,
                      int *max_error, ktime_t *vblank_time,
                      bool in_vblank_irq)
{
    struct vkms_output *output = drm_crtc_to_vkms_output(crtc);
    struct drm_vblank_crtc *vblank = drm_crtc_vblank_crtc(crtc);

    if (!READ_ONCE(vblank->enabled)) {
        *vblank_time = ktime_get();
        return true;
    }

    *vblank_time = READ_ONCE(output->vblank_hrtimer.node.expires);

    if (WARN_ON(*vblank_time == vblank->time))
        return true;

    /*
     * To prevent races we roll the hrtimer forward before we do any
     * interrupt processing - this is how real hw works (the interrupt is
     * only generated after all the vblank registers are updated) and what
     * the vblank core expects. Therefore we need to always correct the
     * timestampe by one frame.
     */
    *vblank_time -= output->period_ns;

    return true;
}
